186-188 Crown, Halifax, Nova Scotia is an existing property with a 2 units property zoned R-2P that allow a 4 units development. The current neglected property has been used as a two units, apartment with first floor 2 bedroom plus den apartment, and rooming apartment in the second level with 3 bedrooms shared accommodation apartment. The property could perhaps be described as the worse property in this well-developed street. There are assorted types of properties from single houses, apartments and condominium types of developments.

The development application is a multiunit 10 Units) for the 6,562 Sq. Ft Lot. The proposal has taken all Land Use Bylaws in considerations and has met all requirements except the amount of units proposed for an 10 units multiunit apartment building in this current R-2P zoning to move towards Multiunit development for a R3 Zoning requirements. The hope is to make this development cost effective. This would allow the possibilities to make a potential affordable housing for the greater public use. Our clients, Mr. Ahmad Abahramian has requested to propose more units for the new development to make sure it is both a project that will help to improve the street as well units that will allow tenants for comfortable spaces as well financially feasible. It is an opportunity to remove/demolish the existing property and start from the ground. The client believe by adding units substantially, it would allow financially make these expenses possible to build as the cost of construction has gone up substantially.

JUBILEE RD.- COLLAGE (Collaborative Aging) PROJECT
*We are looking for high level approval of our concept before moving forward with detailed design.

The site is at the intersection of Jubilee Rd and Preston St. It consists of 3 lots (6258, 6262/64 and 6270 Jubilee) with a total land area of 7,819 sq.ft. (726.4 sq.m). Each of the 3 houses currently on the site has 3 apartment units and in total there are now 27 bedrooms.

This project meets an urgent need for affordable and accessible ageing in place  housing that is missing in Halifax. It is intentionally designed for seniors in the “in-between phase” of needing a smaller home and wanting to downsize, but not yet ready to move into an isolating “care” facility; needing their independence and identity but also wanting to be part of an active and engaging community. 30% of the homes will be affordable.

The proposal is to build a total of 20 homes on the site. Each of the homes has direct and visible access from outside. 19 of the homes have a bedroom, a living area and their own kitchenette (with no 220 wiring). The 20th home on the south east corner of the property has a similar living area, but has 3 bedrooms (intended for students).
All together there are 22 bedrooms.

All of the homes:
-Are accessible and have a front door with direct entry and visibility from outside
-Have access to 2 full shared kitchens-as common space to cook, eat, gather and collaborate
-Have a roll in bathroom and a storage wall
-Fronts onto a south facing courtyard with a community garden and places for outdoor activities ( social, contemplative, fun and green),and
-Have operable windows (light/air) and a balcony or porch/solarium on 2 sides.

The project:
-Designed to fit with the character and quality of the streetscape and a sense of place. A brick exterior, bay windows, and turret influenced by architects from Halifax’s past, specifically, Maxwell who designed the brick townhouses on Jubilee near Robie, Henry Elliot who designed the old Brightwood building, and his brother, who designed Halifax City Hall. The continuity of design familiar to the neighbourhood is crucial to creating a shared sense of place between the new building’s residents and the rest of the community.
- Provides a model for affordable intergenerational living A mix of affordable (30%) homes for seniors as well as two dwellings for students creates an intergenerational, mixed-income community that is able to meet the needs of a diverse range of residents
-Centred around green space, 50% of the site area is open space. The Courtyard is green with porous paving. It is enclosed by a green wall/fence. It serves as an entry area to all of the homes and a place for year round social and recreational activity. Shared amenities like a clothesline, a dog run, a shady spot to sit/, an outdoor barbecue…. encourage, collaboration and community.
-The roof is green. It serves as a social and recreational deck; it has an outdoor edible garden:  and is accessed through and bounded on the south side by a greenhouse/solarium. The greenhouse/solarium makes it possible to be outdoor in most seasons, builds community and increases food security and self-reliance
-The building Includes a small-scale social enterprise off the kitchen that offers high-quality comfort food (soup, sandwiches, salads, sweets and beverages) for the homes as well as serving as a link to the community via a take-out window.
-The design is innovative and targets net zero energy.

Collage offers a model of a place to live where one can continue to have access to what is most important during this still active phase of life, and not be forced to give up so many aspects that support positive social outcomes. The design adheres to the Regional Centre land-use by-law, and is a reflection of the positive, community-building design that can result from the Housing Accelerator Fund-mandated upzoning in urban residential neighbourhoods.

On behalf of our client, Choice Properties REIT, please accept this Development Permit application for a bank (having approximately 446m2 floor area), involving the construction of a new building including a drive-thru. The subject property is located off Portland Street and Eisener Blvd in Dartmouth, NS. The site currently contains a multiple-tenant commercial complex including, Atlantic Superstore, NSLC and Winners. Also located on the subject site, in a separate stand along building, is Camp Bow Wow, a doggie daycare and overnight boarding. The proposed bank will be situated in front of the Atlantic Superstore, currently where the garden centre is located in the parking lot, fronting on Eisener Blvd. 

Based on our review, the site is located in the Dartmouth Plan Area and zoned General Commercial (C-3) under the Land Use By-law (LUB). This zone permits C-2 (General Business) uses which includes business or commercial enterprises and C-1 (Local Business) uses. It is important to note that the Dartmouth LUB does not restrict the subject site to one main building on a lot, in fact, the site currently contains two (2) main buildings on the above noted PID.

Adequate drainage to handle any accidents or spills.
Seating areas for dog owners to supervise their pets.
Water stations for dogs to stay hydrated.
Waste disposal stations for easy cleanup.
Enrichment features such as agility equipment, tunnels, and toys.
Consideration for noise reduction to minimize stress for both dogs and visitors.

JUBILEE RD.- COLLAGE (Collaborative Aging) PROJECT
*We are looking for high level approval of our concept before moving forward with detailed design.

The site is at the intersection of Jubilee Rd and Preston St. It consists of 3 lots (6258, 6262/64 and 6270 Jubilee) with a total land area of 7,819 sq.ft. (726.4 sq.m). Each of the 3 houses currently on the site has 3 apartment units and in total there are now 27 bedrooms.

This project meets an urgent need for affordable and accessible ageing in place  housing that is missing in Halifax. It is intentionally designed for seniors in the “in-between phase” of needing a smaller home and wanting to downsize, but not yet ready to move into an isolating “care” facility; needing their independence and identity but also wanting to be part of an active and engaging community. 30% of the homes will be affordable.

The proposal is to build a total of 20 homes on the site. Each of the homes has direct and visible access from outside. 19 of the homes have a bedroom, a living area and their own kitchenette (with no 220 wiring). The 20th home on the south east corner of the property has a similar living area, but has 3 bedrooms (intended for students).
All together there are 22 bedrooms.

All of the homes:
-Are accessible and have a front door with direct entry and visibility from outside
-Have access to 2 full shared kitchens-as common space to cook, eat, gather and collaborate
-Have a roll in bathroom and a storage wall
-Fronts onto a south facing courtyard with a community garden and places for outdoor activities ( social, contemplative, fun and green),and
-Have operable windows (light/air) and a balcony or porch/solarium on 2 sides.

The project:
-Designed to fit with the character and quality of the streetscape and a sense of place. A brick exterior, bay windows, and turret influenced by architects from Halifax’s past, specifically, Maxwell who designed the brick townhouses on Jubilee near Robie, Henry Elliot who designed the old Brightwood building, and his brother, who designed Halifax City Hall. The continuity of design familiar to the neighbourhood is crucial to creating a shared sense of place between the new building’s residents and the rest of the community.
- Provides a model for affordable intergenerational living A mix of affordable (30%) homes for seniors as well as two dwellings for students creates an intergenerational, mixed-income community that is able to meet the needs of a diverse range of residents
-Centred around green space, 50% of the site area is open space. The Courtyard is green with porous paving. It is enclosed by a green wall/fence. It serves as an entry area to all of the homes and a place for year round social and recreational activity. Shared amenities like a clothesline, a dog run, a shady spot to sit/, an outdoor barbecue…. encourage, collaboration and community.
-The roof is green. It serves as a social and recreational deck; it has an outdoor edible garden:  and is accessed through and bounded on the south side by a greenhouse/solarium. The greenhouse/solarium makes it possible to be outdoor in most seasons, builds community and increases food security and self-reliance
-The building Includes a small-scale social enterprise off the kitchen that offers high-quality comfort food (soup, sandwiches, salads, sweets and beverages) for the homes as well as serving as a link to the community via a take-out window.
-The design is innovative and targets net zero energy.

Collage offers a model of a place to live where one can continue to have access to what is most important during this still active phase of life, and not be forced to give up so many aspects that support positive social outcomes. The design adheres to the Regional Centre land-use by-law, and is a reflection of the positive, community-building design that can result from the Housing Accelerator Fund-mandated upzoning in urban residential neighbourhoods.

Project Overview
The proposed development involves the construction of a 598 square foot addition to the front of the existing detached 2-level residential property. The new addition will be utilized for business purposes. The addition will comply with all zoning requirements, including setback regulations and building height limitations.

Existing Structure:

Building Type: Detached, 2-level
Dimensions: 24.3 x 30.7 ft.
Total Existing Living Area: 1,464 sq. ft.
Parcel Size: 3,680 sq. ft.
PID: 00084095

1. Site Assessment and Preparation
Soil Testing: Perform soil analysis to determine foundation requirements and ensure stability for the new addition.

2. Architectural Design
Design Layout:

The 598 sq. ft. addition will be designed to seamlessly integrate with the existing structure. The design will include commercial-grade features suitable for business operations, such as proper access, lighting, and space functionality.
The design will preserve the property’s aesthetic, with exterior finishes that match or complement the existing structure.

The new addition will comply with the city’s zoning laws, particularly regarding setbacks. The front and flank setbacks will be preserved to align with city regulations.

The maximum allowable height of the addition will not exceed 35 feet as per the city's height restriction guidelines.

3. Construction Process
Foundation and Structural Work:

A reinforced concrete foundation will be poured to support the additional load. Any existing foundation reinforcements required will be identified during the structural analysis.
The framing will be constructed using standard wood framing techniques, with steel reinforcements where necessary to support the additional structure. The exterior will match the current home in material and color.
Roofing will be designed to ensure compatibility with the existing building.

4. Business-Specific Modifications
Signage:
Signage will be designed according to the city's signage ordinances, ensuring it is within allowable size and placement guidelines for business properties.

The addition will comply with all setback and zoning regulations, including those related to height, property line setbacks, and use of the property for business purposes.

The proposed construction will meet the city's building codes, ensuring the safety, stability, and accessibility of the structure.
Permits and Inspections:

The construction process will include required city inspections at various stages of work. The applicant will ensure that all necessary permits are obtained and that the project is fully compliant with city regulations.
Timeline and Budget
Estimated Timeline:

The project is estimated to take approximately 3 months to complete from the start of construction. This includes design, permitting, and final inspections.
Estimated Budget:

A detailed breakdown of the project costs will be provided, including construction, materials, labor, and permits.

Conclusion
This proposal outlines the necessary work for adding a 598 square foot business space to the existing detached home, ensuring compliance with zoning and building regulations. The development aims to create a functional business space while maintaining the integrity of the existing structure. We respectfully request approval for this project from the city.

I'm hoping to add a Secondary Suite to 3169 Ashburn.

This my first time applying for a permit with HRM. The type of permit I've chosen is Development Permit, Type of Work: DEV-Residential and Dwelling - Single Unit. Please let me know if i have made the right choices.

3169 Ashburn Ave is a 1.5 Story Home.
The plan is to turn the unfinished basement into a one bedroom apartment while the main floor and 2nd floor will remain a 3 bedroom unit. 
In addition to adding a secondary suite in the basement, a small amount of work will be done in the primary (upstairs) unit. The addition of an electrical panel, laundry and potentially upgrade some electrical. 
I’m hoping to start this project July 1st, 2021

Attached to this application you will find: 
A site/lot layout, a home elevation drawing, an original basement Layout, a new Layout / Structural drawing, Lighting and power drawing, an Electric Heat, plumbing & Ventilation drawing, 4 pictures of the house/property.

Mechanical: 
The plan starts with upgrading the home’s current 125A Service to a 200A Service w/ two meters feeding a panel each (100A Panels per Unit). A load calculation has been done to support this and a NSP permit will be pulled for all electrical. 
The basement unit will have electric heat and an HRV system. The rest of the house is heated with hot water radiant heat. 
The Furnace and Oil tank location in the basement’s utility room will follow all installation code requirements and will allow for adequate space for maintenance and inspection. 
All plumbing, electrical and Ventilation installation will be completed by a competent, licensed individual.

Layout / structural:
Need to move one load bearing support beam. Will be completed with the help and guidance of stamped structural engineered drawings. 
All Fire separation code rules, barrier free path of travel rules, and window access/egress rules will be followed. 
The ceiling height will be approximately 6’ 8” 
The entrance door and a window will be where the garage (overhead) door is now.

JUBILEE RD.- COLLAGE (Collaborative Aging) PROJECT
*We are looking for high level approval of our concept before moving forward with detailed design.

The site is at the intersection of Jubilee Rd and Preston St. It consists of 3 lots (6258, 6262/64 and 6270 Jubilee) with a total land area of 7,819 sq.ft. (726.4 sq.m). Each of the 3 houses currently on the site has 3 apartment units and in total there are now 27 bedrooms.

This project meets an urgent need for affordable and accessible ageing in place  housing that is missing in Halifax. It is intentionally designed for seniors in the “in-between phase” of needing a smaller home and wanting to downsize, but not yet ready to move into an isolating “care” facility; needing their independence and identity but also wanting to be part of an active and engaging community. 30% of the homes will be affordable.

The proposal is to build a total of 20 homes on the site. Each of the homes has direct and visible access from outside. 19 of the homes have a bedroom, a living area and their own kitchenette (with no 220 wiring). The 20th home on the south east corner of the property has a similar living area, but has 3 bedrooms (intended for students).
All together there are 22 bedrooms.

All of the homes:
-Are accessible and have a front door with direct entry and visibility from outside
-Have access to 2 full shared kitchens-as common space to cook, eat, gather and collaborate
-Have a roll in bathroom and a storage wall
-Fronts onto a south facing courtyard with a community garden and places for outdoor activities ( social, contemplative, fun and green),and
-Have operable windows (light/air) and a balcony or porch/solarium on 2 sides.

The project:
-Designed to fit with the character and quality of the streetscape and a sense of place. A brick exterior, bay windows, and turret influenced by architects from Halifax’s past, specifically, Maxwell who designed the brick townhouses on Jubilee near Robie, Henry Elliot who designed the old Brightwood building, and his brother, who designed Halifax City Hall. The continuity of design familiar to the neighbourhood is crucial to creating a shared sense of place between the new building’s residents and the rest of the community.
- Provides a model for affordable intergenerational living A mix of affordable (30%) homes for seniors as well as two dwellings for students creates an intergenerational, mixed-income community that is able to meet the needs of a diverse range of residents
-Centred around green space, 50% of the site area is open space. The Courtyard is green with porous paving. It is enclosed by a green wall/fence. It serves as an entry area to all of the homes and a place for year round social and recreational activity. Shared amenities like a clothesline, a dog run, a shady spot to sit/, an outdoor barbecue…. encourage, collaboration and community.
-The roof is green. It serves as a social and recreational deck; it has an outdoor edible garden:  and is accessed through and bounded on the south side by a greenhouse/solarium. The greenhouse/solarium makes it possible to be outdoor in most seasons, builds community and increases food security and self-reliance
-The building Includes a small-scale social enterprise off the kitchen that offers high-quality comfort food (soup, sandwiches, salads, sweets and beverages) for the homes as well as serving as a link to the community via a take-out window.
-The design is innovative and targets net zero energy.

Collage offers a model of a place to live where one can continue to have access to what is most important during this still active phase of life, and not be forced to give up so many aspects that support positive social outcomes. The design adheres to the Regional Centre land-use by-law, and is a reflection of the positive, community-building design that can result from the Housing Accelerator Fund-mandated upzoning in urban residential neighbourhoods.
